[05/02/26, 1:01:44 AM] Buddankay: Dear Students, 

Greetings.

As you all know, at the end of the semester, all students will be doing their Apparel Internship duration summer.
Duration of the Apparel Internship: 8 weeks - from 1st June to 24th July 2026.


Please also note the following important points.
The duration of the Internship will be 8 weeks, from 1st June to 25th July as per the revised academic calendar.
The number of students per unit is maximum two.
If the same company has 3-4 units, you may complete the internship with the same company; however, you must ensure there are no more than 2 students in the same unit. you may do Internship in different units.
Student must clearly mention to industry that Apparel internship is mainly production internship in which students are supposed to cover all departments along with all mandatory annexures.
Pls avoid a kind of industry which only assign Internship to specific department only. 
For the industry-assigned project/assignment, Students may be required to do individual work if the industry wants them same way.
In that case, your basic industry department study will be common, while the assignment work will be individual.
Annexures are mandatory and must be completed individually. 

Objectives of Apparel Internship:
 Relate classroom teaching with industry practices followed by apparel manufacturers;
 Gain practical exposure to the process flows and hierarchies;
 Gain practical knowledge on machinery and equipment used in apparel manufacturing;
 Exposure to production systems, measures to improve productivity, quality management principles employed in the plant atmosphere;Employing problem solving skills learned during the course and attempt to find solutions to minor problems faced by the factory.
 Improve interpersonal skills by constant communication with factory personnel.
 Gain networking skills by interacting with managers in the industry.
 Experiment on possible real-life projects and understand research/problem-solving approaches suitable for apparel manufacturing.

Broader theme of activities to be undertaken during Apparel Internship:
 Understand the process flow of a factory/plant through observation and questioning.
 Understand the life cycle of an order from its inception to the shipment of the order through observation and interviewing.
 Document the functions of different departments with objectives of each department, the structure of the department, roles of each job title,job requirements.
 Interact and study the role of supervisors in each department, mainly cutting, sewing & finishing;
 Performing department study for other departments in case of organizations having additional departments like Embroidery, Washing, Printing etc.;
 Exploring standard operating procedures followed by the plant;
 Looking at the extent of usage of Information Systems in different departments, including MIS, ERP software & Real time data collection systems;
 Gaining hands-on experience in operating machinery and tools;
 Executing any area or topic or assignment to provide solutions if the organization wishes the skills of the student to be utilized in a fitting manner;
 Collection of formats used for data collection, Fabric swatches, trim swatches, and tech-packs to be used by the student for documentation purposes 
    (with due permission from the company officials).

Mandatory Studies to be conducted during Apparel Internship:
 Time study and subsequent capacity calculations for all departments, including spreading, cutting, fusing, ticketing, bundling, sewing,washing, embroidery, finishing & packing wherever applicable. (For deeper understanding of time standards the students will have to perform time study and capacity studies for a minimum of 2 styles or 2 different fabric types per student).
 Analyze and derive OB (Operation Breakdown) for all operations of one style in the sewing room.
 Conduct time study for all operations for a minimum of one style and establish time standards.
 Find out the ratio between working & not working through work sampling and analyze the causes for idle time in the production floor.
 Layout study with respect to machinery placement, material movement, auxiliary services placement etc.
 Document the working environment including the type of building, building features including the shed type, window sizes, ventilation,lighting, types of floor.
 Study of Information systems being followed (MIS, ERP etc) and study of CAD systems followed.
 Allotting of orders against factory capacity.
 Filling up data collection formats provided by the institute.

Additional studies that can be conducted:
 Rating of sewing operators
 Preparation of skill matrix
 Preparation of cut order plan
 Line balancing
 Operator training
 Packing list preparation during shipment (If the organization gives permission)
 Shipment and final inspection procedures (If the organization gives permission)
 Study of quality requirements by different brands/buyers
 Material Handling Systems
 Embroidery design software
 Preparation of the cost sheet (If the organization gives permission)
 Application of Lean Manufacturing Practices
 Development of databases as per the organization's requirement

The Internship report prepared by students will be kept confidential for academic and research purposes and will not be displayed in the library.
Deliverables
 On the job training gained during internship period;
 Internship document prepared and to be submitted by the student or student group with proper format including the copies of certification from the organization;
 The document should cover all the aspects of day to day organizational activities and include all the studies conducted;
 Formats and data collected should be part of annexure;
 Outcome and learning of the internship.

Student Discipline during Apparel Internship:
 Students should adhere to the schedule as advised by the institute.
 Report every day to the plant as per the rules of the organization and leave as per the timings provided by the organization.
 In the event of the student being tasked with certain duties related to any of the departments, the students are expected to carry out the activities to the best of their abilities to get on the job experience.

As mentioned in the Apparel Internship guideline, mandatory activities, work sampling is one of the mandatory activities.
For your reference, you are all supposed to coverthe annexures mentioned below.

Annexure   XI

Work Sampling


Work sampling to find out the ratio between working & not working through work sampling and 
analyze the causes of idle time on the production floor. Please find the attached guideline and format for the Work Sampling.
" If the factories use automatic spreading and cutting machines, students should carry out the work sampling of such machines and their operators. 
   The rest of the students may be advised to carry out the work sampling of sewing floor manpower (e.g. one specific line)"

[15/03/26, 12:52:45 AM] Buddankay: Natural Dyes in Indian Textile Tradition

India has a rich heritage of natural dyeing techniques. Traditional textile crafts such as block printing, tie-dye, and handloom weaving use natural dyes.

Examples include:

Indigo dyeing in Rajasthan

Kalamkari printing in Andhra Pradesh

Ajrakh printing in Gujarat

These techniques use plant-based dyes and traditional methods passed down through generations.

14. Role of Natural Dyes in Sustainable Fashion

Sustainable fashion focuses on reducing environmental impact in clothing production. Natural dyes support this goal by providing eco-friendly alternatives to synthetic dyes.

Fashion brands are increasingly adopting natural dyes in their collections to promote sustainability.

Consumers are also becoming more conscious about environmentally friendly clothing.

15. Recent Innovations in Natural Dyeing

Modern research is improving natural dyeing techniques.

Some innovations include:

Microwave dye extraction

Enzyme-assisted dyeing

Bio-mordants

Microbial dye production

These technologies help improve colour fastness and efficiency.

16. Environmental Impact

Natural dyes have lower environmental impact compared to synthetic dyes.

They reduce chemical pollution and wastewater toxicity. However, sustainable cultivation of dye plants is necessary to avoid overexploitation of natural resources.

17. Economic Importance

Natural dye production can create employment opportunities in rural areas. Cultivation of dye plants and traditional dyeing practices support small-scale industries and artisans.

18. Challenges in Commercialization

Several challenges exist in the commercial production of natural dyes:

High production cost

Limited raw material supply

Lack of standardization

Competition from synthetic dyes

Addressing these challenges is important for wider adoption.

19. Future Scope

The future of natural dyes is promising due to growing demand for sustainable fashion. Research and technological innovations are helping improve dye performance and expand colour range.

Government support and consumer awareness can further promote the use of natural dyes.

20. Conclusion

Natural dyes are an important component of sustainable textile production. They offer eco-friendly alternatives to synthetic dyes and support traditional textile practices.

Although natural dyes have certain limitations, technological advancements and increased environmental awareness are encouraging their revival in the modern apparel industry.

Promoting natural dyeing practices can help create a more sustainable and environmentally responsible textile sector.

[15/03/26, 1:03:59 AM] Buddankay: Natural Dyes in the Indian Tradition of Textile Production

The use of dyes made from natural sources has been an important part of India's textile industry for centuries. Historically, dyes obtained from plants such as indigo, turmeric, madder and pomegranate were used to colour fabrics made from cotton, silk or wool. These methods of dyeing were passed on through generations by experienced artisans.

Many traditional methods of textile production in India (block printing, tie-dying and hand weaving) still use natural dyes today and are part of India's cultural heritage and history of textile production.

Examples of Indian Natural Dyeing Traditions include:

Indigo dyeing - the production of blue hues using natural indigo derived from the leaves of plants.

Kalamkari - traditional hand-painted and block-printed designs which are coloured with natural dyes derived from plants or minerals.

Ajrakh printing - a traditional method of block printing that uses natural dyes derived from indigo and madder to form intricate designs.

The continuation of traditional processes of dyeing foster sustainable textile production and also assist in the preservation of India's textile production heritage.
[15/03/26, 1:06:46 AM] Buddankay: The Indian textile tradition has a long history of using natural dyes for textiles. For thousands of years, India has produced textiles with natural colors from sources such as madder, turmeric, indigo, and pomegranate. Artisans who used these dyeing techniques learned how to create color through an apprenticeship process over time.


The use of natural dyes for traditional textile crafts like handloom weaving, block printing, and tie-dye is very common in many areas of India. These crafts provide India with cultural continuity while developing textile history.


Types of natural dyeing/
Indigo/Indigo dyeing - creates blue shades from the indigo plant.
Kalamkari /is a method of hand painting or block printing with natural dyes from plants or minerals.
Ajrak/Ajrakh printing - is a traditional method of block printing with natural dyes such as indigo and madder to create very detailed designs.


These traditional dying processes encourage sustainable textile production while keeping India's textile heritage alive.

[15/03/26, 1:09:18 AM] Buddankay: Sustainable fashion is the term used to describe the efforts that aim to minimize the adverse impact of clothing production on the environment. Sustainable fashion encourages the use of environmentally friendly practices. In this context, the use of natural dyes is significant. This is because natural dyes act as a more environmentally friendly alternative to synthetic dyes, which may contain harmful chemicals. As natural dyes are obtained from natural resources, they are biodegradable and do not harm the environment. In recent years, many fashion brands have started to use natural dyes in their clothing as part of their sustainable fashion practices. In addition, consumers have become more conscious of the environmental impact of their actions. They have become more interested in clothing that is made from sustainable and natural resources. Therefore, the use of natural dyes is becoming more significant in the context of sustainable fashion.
‎[15/03/26, 1:09:43 AM] Lalith: ‎image omitted
[15/03/26, 1:12:31 AM] Buddankay: Natural dyes are also considered to have fewer impacts on the environment compared to synthetic dyes used in the textile industry. Most synthetic dyes contain certain chemicals that are not only harmful to the environment but can also contaminate water resources if they are not properly managed during the process of textile production. On the contrary, natural dyes are easy to use in the textile industry since they are not only biodegradable but also derived from renewable resources. Therefore, it can be concluded that natural dyes are more beneficial to the environment than synthetic dyes. However, it should be noted that natural resources should be used in an effective manner to avoid environmental degradation.
[15/03/26, 1:12:53 AM] Buddankay: Natural dye markets and natural dyes are economically beneficial to rural areas and small scale businesses. Many communities traditionally cultivate dye plants to manufacture natural dyes. These activities promote job creation for farmers, artists/designers and small scale textile producers and contribute to job creation. Additionally, the increasing demand for environmentally and sustainably produced products has made naturally dyed textiles more desirable to consumers in local, national and international markets. Through supporting the production of natural dyes, the textile industry can also help to keep traditional skills alive and provide a source of income to local communities.
[15/03/26, 1:14:40 AM] Buddankay: The production of natural dyes can be a contributor to the economic development of rural areas. Natural dyes are produced by growing plants for dye production. This creates employment for people living in these regions. The need for sustainable and eco-friendly products has led to an increased interest in naturally dyed textiles. This has created a new market for naturally dyed products. The textile industry can be a contributor to the preservation of traditional practices by supporting the production of natural dyes.
[15/03/26, 1:16:09 AM] Buddankay: Despite the many advantages of natural dyes, there are a number of challenges that face the use of natural dyes. One of the challenges is the availability of the raw materials. Natural dyes are based on the use of agricultural products. Another challenge is the production of consistent colors. Different natural dyes may vary with the quality of the plants used. In addition, the process of natural dyeing is time-consuming compared to synthetic dyeing. All these challenges may result in high costs, which may hinder the textile industry from competing with synthetic dyes.
[15/03/26, 1:17:58 AM] Buddankay: The future of natural dyes in the textile industry appears to be quite promising as the issue of sustainability is becoming a major concern in the fashion and clothing industry. Researchers and textile experts are working towards the development of better techniques that will improve the efficiency of natural dyes. In addition, the growing concern over the protection of the environment and the use of sustainable fashion is contributing to the use of natural dye products. Therefore, with the growing research and technological advancements, the efficiency of natural dyes may improve.
[15/03/26, 1:21:37 AM] Buddankay: Natural dyes play an important role in promoting sustainable practices in the textile and apparel industry. They are derived from renewable natural resources and are generally safer for the environment compared to synthetic dyes. Natural dyeing also supports traditional textile crafts and provides economic opportunities for artisans and rural communities. Although there are challenges related to colour consistency, production cost, and raw material availability, ongoing research and growing consumer awareness are helping to revive the use of natural dyes. Promoting natural dyeing techniques can contribute to a more environmentally responsible and sustainable fashion industry.

[16/03/26, 12:58:35 AM] Lalith: The research paper titled “A Review on Concerns for Apparel Shopping: A Study of Plus-Size Customers” discusses the growing importance of the plus-size apparel market and the difficulties faced by customers while purchasing ready-made clothing. Plus-size clothing refers to garments designed for individuals whose body sizes are larger than the standard clothing sizes. In recent years, the Indian fashion industry has experienced rapid growth, and the plus-size segment has emerged as an important niche market. Many retailers and fashion brands have started introducing collections specifically designed for plus-size consumers. Despite this development, many customers still face several challenges such as limited size availability, poor fitting garments, lack of trendy designs, and higher prices compared to regular clothing. These issues make shopping for clothing more difficult for people who require larger sizes.

The study mainly reviews previous research and articles to understand the buying behavior and concerns of plus-size consumers. Several factors influence clothing purchase decisions, including comfort, quality, color, design, price, and brand reputation. Store environment and customer service also play a role in shaping the shopping experience. Psychological aspects such as self-esteem and body image are especially important for plus-size consumers because clothing that fits properly can increase confidence and satisfaction. Many studies also highlight that most brands focus more on women’s plus-size clothing, while the men’s plus-size segment often receives less attention. In addition, consumers today are more aware of fashion trends and prefer clothing that is both stylish and comfortable.

The findings of the study indicate that although the plus-size apparel market is expanding, there are still several issues that need to be addressed. Customers frequently struggle to find well-fitting and fashionable clothes that match their body shape and personal style. In recent years, some homegrown brands and online retailers have started offering better options, including customized clothing and extended size ranges. These efforts help improve confidence and satisfaction among plus-size consumers. The study concludes that retailers and designers must focus more on inclusivity, accurate sizing systems, and fashionable designs in order to meet the needs of this growing consumer segment and provide a better overall shopping experience.
[16/03/26, 12:59:55 AM] Buddankay: faridabad paper
[16/03/26, 1:00:23 AM] Lalith: The research paper titled “A Study on Factors Influencing Plus Size Fashion Apparel” examines the factors that influence the purchasing behavior and satisfaction of plus-size clothing consumers in India. Plus-size clothing refers to garments designed for individuals who require larger sizes than the standard clothing range. The study highlights that the plus-size apparel market was neglected for many years but has recently gained attention due to the increasing demand for fashionable clothing for larger body types. Customer satisfaction plays an important role in the clothing consumption process, which includes purchasing, using, storing, and eventually discarding apparel. The study explains that many plus-size consumers feel dissatisfied with clothing due to issues such as poor fitting, lack of style, limited variety, and fewer alternatives available in the market.

The research uses both quantitative and qualitative approaches to understand consumer behavior. Data was collected using questionnaires distributed to several respondents in order to analyze their shopping satisfaction, buying satisfaction, and consuming satisfaction. The study examined how these factors influence the decision of plus-size customers to wear, keep, or discard clothing. The results show that many consumers purchase plus-size clothing based on fashion trends, color choices, comfort, fitting, and brand reputation. According to the findings, a large percentage of respondents prefer fashionable and trendy clothing, and they often visit specialized plus-size stores because regular stores do not provide suitable options. However, some consumers feel hesitant to purchase plus-size clothing due to privacy concerns and fear of body shaming while shopping in stores.

The study concludes that factors such as fashion trends, variety of clothing, comfort, pricing, and store environment significantly influence the buying decisions of plus-size consumers. Many respondents prefer customized clothing rather than ready-made garments because it provides better fitting and comfort. The research also found that the price of plus-size clothing is often higher than regular clothing, which negatively affects customer satisfaction. Therefore, retailers and designers should focus on offering a wider variety of fashionable designs, affordable pricing, and better store layouts that respect customer privacy. By improving these aspects, companies can better serve the growing plus-size fashion market and increase customer satisfaction in this segment.
[16/03/26, 1:01:14 AM] Lalith: The dissertation “Evaluating the Plus Size Body: Advertisement and Exploitation in the Media and the Fashion Industry” examines how plus-size bodies are represented in the media and fashion industries. The research explores whether the inclusion of plus-size models in advertisements is a genuine attempt to promote body diversity or simply a marketing strategy used by brands to attract attention and profit. In recent years, the visibility of plus-size bodies in advertising and social media has increased, but the study questions whether this representation is meaningful or only superficial. The research focuses on how brands present plus-size models in advertising campaigns, websites, and social media platforms to understand whether their actions truly support body inclusivity.

The study also discusses how media and consumer culture have historically shaped ideas about the “ideal body.” For many years, the fashion industry promoted extremely thin body types as the standard of beauty. Over time, body trends changed and curvier bodies began to appear more frequently in media and popular culture. However, the research argues that even when curvier bodies are represented, they often still reflect a modified version of the thin ideal rather than true body diversity. Advertising, magazines, and social media platforms play a major role in spreading these body ideals, which can influence how women perceive their own bodies and encourage them to compare themselves with unrealistic images.

The dissertation concludes that although plus-size representation in fashion and media has improved, it is still limited and sometimes misleading. Many brands include plus-size models in their campaigns but fail to provide clothing in extended sizes or do not consistently promote body diversity in their products. In some cases, companies use the body positivity movement mainly as a marketing tool rather than a genuine effort to support inclusivity. The research suggests that greater transparency and more authentic representation of diverse body types are needed in the fashion industry so that plus-size individuals can feel truly included and represented in media and advertising.
[16/03/26, 1:03:08 AM] Lalith: The research article “One Size Does Not Fit All: Optimizing Size-Inclusive Model Photography Mitigates Fit Risk in Online Fashion Retailing” examines how the body size of fashion models used in online shopping websites affects consumers’ purchasing decisions. The study explains that most online fashion retailers still use very thin models to display clothing items. However, these images often fail to represent the body shapes of many consumers. This situation can negatively influence how shoppers perceive their own bodies and may also affect their confidence when buying clothes online. Since customers cannot physically try on garments when shopping online, they rely heavily on model images to imagine how the clothes might look on them. The research therefore focuses on how different model body sizes influence customers’ perception of clothing fit and their willingness to purchase products.

The study introduces the concept called the “Dissimilarity-Risk Deterrence Effect.” This idea explains that when consumers see clothing displayed on models whose body size is very different from their own, they feel less confident about how the clothing will fit them. As a result, they perceive a higher risk of poor fit and become less likely to purchase the product. Through multiple studies and experiments, the researchers found that when clothing is shown on models with body sizes similar to the consumer’s own body size, customers feel more confident in judging the product fit. This similarity helps them imagine how the clothing might look on their body, which reduces uncertainty and increases purchase intention.

The findings suggest that using diverse body sizes in fashion model photography can benefit both consumers and retailers. Showing clothing on models of different sizes can make online shopping more inclusive and help customers make better purchasing decisions. It can also reduce product returns, which often occur when customers receive clothing that does not fit as expected. Therefore, the study concludes that fashion brands and online retailers should adopt size-inclusive marketing strategies by displaying clothing on models with varied body types. This approach can improve customer satisfaction, increase sales, and promote a more inclusive representation of body diversity in the fashion industry.
[16/03/26, 1:06:27 AM] Lalith: The study titled “Exploring Influential Factors on Customer Satisfaction with Clothing for Overweight and Obese Women: A Focus on Generations Y and Z in Sri Lanka” examines the factors that influence clothing satisfaction among overweight and obese women aged between 20 and 35 years in Sri Lanka. The global textile and clothing industry has grown rapidly, and the demand for plus-size clothing is increasing as the number of overweight and obese individuals rises worldwide. Despite this growing demand, many fashion retailers still provide limited options for plus-size women, leading to dissatisfaction related to clothing fit, style, and availability. The study highlights that clothing plays an important role in shaping self-confidence, identity, and social perception. However, women with larger body sizes often struggle to find fashionable clothing that fits well, which can lead to frustration and feelings of exclusion from the fashion industry.

To understand the determinants of clothing satisfaction, the research is based on Renoux’s theory of retail satisfaction, which includes three major components: the shopping system, buying system, and consuming system. The shopping system focuses on aspects before purchase, such as store accessibility, proper information about plus-size clothing, and the presence of mannequins that represent larger body types. The buying system involves factors during the purchasing process, including the availability of different sizes, stylish designs, reasonable price ranges, high-quality materials, and spacious fitting rooms. The consuming system refers to the experience after purchase, including body proportion, body image, comfort, and how well the clothing fits the consumer’s body. The study collected data using a questionnaire from overweight and obese women across different provinces in Sri Lanka and analyzed the results through statistical techniques such as multiple regression analysis.

The findings reveal that the shopping system and buying system have a significant positive influence on clothing satisfaction among overweight and obese women, with the buying system having the strongest impact. Factors such as having a separate section for plus-size clothing, displaying plus-size mannequins, offering trendy designs, maintaining reasonable prices, and providing comfortable fitting rooms can greatly improve customer satisfaction. However, the consuming system was found to have no significant statistical influence on satisfaction in this study. The research concludes that fashion retailers should pay greater attention to the needs of plus-size consumers by improving store environments, offering diverse and fashionable clothing options, and ensuring better accessibility and information. These improvements can enhance the shopping experience and help plus-size women feel more confident, comfortable, and included in the fashion market.
[16/03/26, 1:07:34 AM] Lalith: The thesis “Body Inclusivity: The Effect of Size Availability on the Well-Being of Different Size Young Women” examines how clothing size availability in the fashion industry influences young women’s emotions, body image, and overall well-being. The study explains that clothing is closely connected to a person’s body image and self-perception. When young women cannot find clothes that fit their bodies, they may feel dissatisfied with themselves and believe that their bodies do not match society’s “ideal” appearance. Fashion advertisements often promote thin and attractive models, which can create unrealistic beauty standards. As a result, many young women compare themselves with these idealized images and may develop lower self-esteem, negative emotions, and dissatisfaction with their body shape.

The research also highlights the growing importance of body positivity and size inclusivity in the fashion industry. The body positivity movement encourages acceptance of different body shapes and challenges unrealistic beauty ideals promoted by media and fashion brands. However, even though many companies claim to support body inclusivity, true representation is still limited. Many clothing brands offer only a narrow range of sizes, and plus-size options are often unavailable in physical stores. The study discusses how inclusive sizing and representation in advertising can influence consumers’ feelings of belonging, emotional responses, and purchasing decisions. Young women are more likely to feel included and confident when they see models with body types similar to their own and when clothing sizes are available for a wider range of body shapes.

To investigate these effects, the researcher conducted a quantitative study using an experimental design where participants were shown different models (thin and plus-size) and different levels of size availability (inclusive and exclusive). The results indicated that the similarity between consumers and the models had a significant influence on purchase intention. When consumers felt that the model’s body type was similar to their own and when inclusive sizes were available, they showed a greater willingness to purchase the clothing. Although some expected effects such as emotional response and well-being were not statistically significant, the research still emphasizes the importance of size inclusivity in improving shopping experiences and consumer satisfaction. The study concludes that fashion brands should expand size ranges and represent diverse body types to promote inclusivity and improve the confidence and well-being of young women.
[16/03/26, 1:08:55 AM] Lalith: ‎You deleted this message.
[16/03/26, 1:10:33 AM] Lalith: Gemini said
The following essay summarizes the challenges and evolving opportunities within India’s plus-size fashion market:

Introduction: The Push for Inclusivity
India’s fashion landscape is currently navigating a complex intersection of traditional beauty standards and a growing global body positivity movement. While the demand for plus-size clothing is surging, many consumers continue to face significant frustration due to inconsistent sizing, societal biases, and a lack of mainstream representation. Despite these hurdles, the industry is at a pivotal moment where inclusivity is moving from a niche trend toward a core business principle.

Market Growth and Economic Potential
The economic outlook for the plus-size sector is robust. Market analysis indicates the global plus-size clothing market was valued at approximately USD 311.44 billion in 2023, with projections suggesting it will reach USD 412.39 billion by 2030. While North America currently holds the largest revenue share, India is emerging as a critical player. This domestic growth is fueled by shifting societal attitudes and technological innovations, such as advanced sizing algorithms that improve the digital shopping experience.

Drivers of Change: Gen Z and Social Media
The demand for meaningful change is largely driven by younger Indian shoppers, particularly Gen Z, who vocalize their preference for brands that celebrate body diversity. Social media has acted as a powerful amplifier for these values; discussions regarding body positivity on platforms like Instagram and Facebook recently saw a 43% year-over-year surge. In response, brands like Jivika and The Plus-Size Store have launched campaigns centered on self-love and diverse modeling.

Persistent Industry Challenges
Despite optimistic growth figures, the industry faces deep-seated obstacles. Cultural ideals in India are heavily influenced by Bollywood’s preference for slender archetypes, which often casts larger bodies in a negative light. This bias is reflected in retail practices where plus-size options are often limited to online inventories or niche sections. Furthermore, representation in high fashion remains stagnant; a 2025 report found that a mere 0.8% of looks across major global fashion weeks featured plus-size models. Even when inclusive ranges are offered, inconsistent sizing continues to alienate shoppers.

The Digital Divide and Regional Disparities
E-commerce has become a vital lifeline for plus-size consumers, offering extensive size ranges and virtual try-on tools that physical stores often lack. However, a significant gap remains between urban and rural areas. In tier-2 and tier-3 cities, traditional beauty norms remain firm, and physical retail options for plus-size shoppers are scarce. While digital platforms provide a solution, access is sometimes limited by connectivity and awareness in these smaller regions.

Conclusion
For India’s fashion industry to truly embrace inclusivity, it must move beyond superficial marketing and address fundamental issues like standardized sizing and diverse representation in high-profile media. Brands that successfully navigate these cultural and logistical hurdles stand to build deep loyalty with a new generation of consumers who prioritize authenticity. Ultimately, the future of the market depends on reimagining beauty standards to ensure fashion is accessible to all body types.

[16/03/26, 1:22:37 AM] Lalith: The reviewed studies collectively examine the growing importance of the plus-size fashion market and the challenges faced by consumers when purchasing clothing designed for larger body sizes. Plus-size apparel refers to garments created for individuals whose body measurements exceed standard sizing ranges. In recent years, the demand for plus-size clothing has increased significantly, particularly in countries like India where the fashion industry is expanding rapidly. As awareness of body diversity grows, fashion brands and retailers have begun introducing collections designed specifically for plus-size consumers. However, despite this progress, many customers still experience difficulties when searching for suitable clothing.

Several studies highlight that plus-size consumers often face problems such as limited size availability, poor garment fitting, lack of fashionable designs, and higher prices compared to regular clothing. These issues make the shopping experience more challenging and often lead to dissatisfaction among customers. Many consumers report that clothing available in stores does not properly match their body proportions, which affects both comfort and confidence. As a result, some customers prefer customized clothing rather than ready-made garments because it offers better fit and comfort.

Consumer purchasing decisions for plus-size clothing are influenced by various factors including comfort, quality, color, style, price, and brand reputation. In addition, the store environment and customer service play an important role in shaping the shopping experience. Psychological factors such as body image and self-esteem are also significant because clothing that fits well can improve confidence and personal satisfaction. Research also suggests that while the women’s plus-size market has gained more attention, the men’s plus-size clothing segment still remains relatively underdeveloped.

Another important aspect discussed in the literature is the representation of plus-size bodies in media and advertising. Historically, the fashion industry has promoted thin body types as the ideal standard of beauty. Although the presence of plus-size models in advertisements and social media has increased in recent years, some researchers argue that this representation is sometimes used as a marketing strategy rather than a genuine effort to promote body inclusivity. Many brands include plus-size models in campaigns but do not consistently offer extended clothing sizes or diverse body representations in their products.

Research related to online fashion retailing emphasizes the importance of using models with diverse body types in product displays. When consumers see clothing modeled by individuals with body sizes similar to their own, they feel more confident about how the garment might fit them. This concept, known as the “dissimilarity-risk deterrence effect,” explains that customers are less likely to purchase clothing when the model’s body type is very different from their own. Therefore, displaying clothing on models of different sizes can improve purchase intention and reduce uncertainty in online shopping.

Studies focusing on customer satisfaction among overweight and obese women also show that factors related to the shopping and buying experience significantly influence satisfaction levels. Elements such as separate sections for plus-size clothing, the presence of plus-size mannequins, availability of fashionable designs, reasonable pricing, and comfortable fitting rooms contribute positively to the overall shopping experience. When these factors are properly addressed, consumers feel more comfortable, confident, and included in the fashion market.

Overall, the literature suggests that although the plus-size apparel market is expanding and gaining recognition, several improvements are still required within the fashion industry. Retailers and designers need to focus on inclusive sizing systems, wider variety of fashionable designs, fair pricing, and better representation of diverse body types in both retail spaces and media. By addressing these concerns, the fashion industry can create a more inclusive environment that improves customer satisfaction, confidence, and overall well-being for plus-size consumers.

[16/03/26, 1:32:26 AM] Buddankay: India’s fashion industry is slowly confronting the reality that style should not be limited by body size. Although the global body positivity movement has encouraged brands to embrace diverse body types, progress in India remains uneven. Many plus-size consumers still struggle to find fashionable clothing that fits well due to limited size ranges, poor design considerations, and deeply rooted societal biases about ideal body shapes. Cultural influences—especially media and entertainment industries that promote slimmer body standards—continue to shape public perceptions of beauty. As a result, plus-size shoppers often feel excluded from mainstream fashion spaces, even though clothing plays an important role in expressing identity and confidence in Indian culture.

At the same time, market trends reveal strong potential for growth in the plus-size segment. Globally, the plus-size clothing industry is expanding steadily, and India is emerging as a promising market due to rising awareness about body diversity and changing consumer attitudes. Younger shoppers, particularly Gen Z, are actively advocating for size inclusivity and supporting brands that represent real bodies. Social media has amplified these conversations, allowing influencers and everyday consumers to challenge outdated beauty standards. E-commerce platforms have also made it easier for people to access extended sizes and explore brands that promote inclusivity. However, despite these positive developments, representation in high fashion, runways, and advertising campaigns remains limited, and inconsistent sizing continues to frustrate many customers.

Looking ahead, the future of India’s plus-size fashion market holds both challenges and opportunities. Brands that genuinely embrace inclusivity—by offering better sizing systems, diverse representation, and thoughtfully designed clothing—have the potential to build strong connections with consumers. Online retail, technological innovations such as virtual try-ons, and collaborations with body-positive influencers are already helping some brands reach wider audiences. Yet meaningful change will require more than marketing campaigns; it demands a shift in industry mindset and cultural attitudes toward body diversity. If designers, retailers, and media platforms work together to normalize inclusive fashion, the industry can move toward a future where style, confidence, and self-expression are accessible to everyone, regardless of body size.

[16/03/26, 1:34:46 AM] Buddankay: At a recent edition of Lakmé Fashion Week, the presence of curvier models and body-positivity advocates on the runway signaled a small but meaningful shift in India’s fashion landscape. Influencers and creators walked for well-known designers in stylish outfits that moved beyond the stereotypical oversized silhouettes usually associated with plus-size fashion. While this representation appeared encouraging, it also raised an important question: is the industry truly embracing inclusivity, or are these appearances simply symbolic gestures? Despite growing discussions around body positivity, the Indian fashion industry still struggles to fully integrate size inclusivity into its design and retail practices. This is particularly surprising considering that a large portion of Indian consumers fall into the plus-size category, making it a significant and potentially profitable market.

Plus-size consumers in India are increasingly vocal about what they want from fashion. Rather than loose or shapeless garments, many seek trendy, well-fitted clothing that reflects global fashion trends and allows them to confidently express their personal style. Brands such as Half Full Curve have attempted to fill this gap by offering fashionable options specifically designed for curvier bodies. However, experts argue that many designers still misunderstand the needs of this market, often producing limited silhouettes or basic designs instead of a full range of stylish choices. Another major issue is the absence of a standardized Indian sizing chart. Since many designers rely on Western size measurements that do not reflect typical Indian body proportions, customers frequently face inconsistent fits and confusion when shopping.

Beyond design challenges, plus-size shoppers often encounter practical and emotional barriers while purchasing clothing. Many report being charged higher prices for larger sizes—a practice widely criticized as “fat tax”—while others experience uncomfortable or judgmental treatment in physical stores. Limited trial samples and the lack of size-diverse models in campaigns further make it difficult for customers to visualize how garments will look on them. Although some designers and platforms are trying to improve the experience by offering extended sizes, custom measurements, and inclusive campaigns, experts emphasize that real change requires deeper commitment. True inclusivity will only emerge when designers prioritize diverse body shapes during the design process itself, create welcoming retail environments, and treat size diversity as a natural part of fashion rather than a temporary marketing trend.
[16/03/26, 1:35:07 AM] Buddankay: India’s fashion industry is gradually moving toward a more inclusive approach as size-inclusive clothing becomes more visible and accessible. What was once a neglected segment is now gaining attention as shoppers increasingly look for garments that fit real bodies rather than limited standard sizes. Across cities like Bengaluru, Mumbai, and Delhi, many consumers are experiencing the relief of finally finding stylish outfits that accommodate diverse body shapes. The broader fashion and lifestyle sector in India is already a massive market, and the rapid growth of online shopping is further accelerating change. As awareness about body positivity spreads, brands are beginning to recognize that inclusivity is not only socially important but also a major business opportunity.

The demand for better sizing and fit is driven by evolving consumer expectations. Many shoppers report that poor fit and inconsistent sizing are among the biggest barriers when purchasing clothing. In a country known for its cultural diversity and wide range of traditional garments—from sarees to lehengas and kurtas—designing clothes that suit different body types becomes even more essential. E-commerce platforms have played a crucial role in expanding access, especially for consumers in Tier-II and Tier-III cities who previously had limited options in physical stores. Through online marketplaces and social media, shoppers are discovering brands that offer extended sizes and more thoughtful designs, helping them feel represented in the fashion space.

Despite this progress, the journey toward true size inclusivity still faces several challenges. Producing clothing in a wider range of sizes often increases manufacturing complexity and costs, while cultural attitudes in some regions can still discourage open acceptance of plus-size fashion. New brands must also work harder to build trust among customers who are accustomed to well-known labels. However, the opportunities remain significant. With the plus-size market steadily expanding and digital platforms making fashion more accessible across the country, brands that prioritize consistent sizing, comfort, and authentic representation are likely to gain loyal customers. As India’s fashion industry continues to evolve, size-inclusive design has the potential to redefine how people experience clothing—making fashion not just about trends, but about confidence, comfort, and belonging for everyone.
[16/03/26, 1:35:49 AM] Buddankay: The global plus-size clothing market is experiencing steady expansion as consumer attitudes toward body diversity and inclusive fashion continue to evolve. Valued at around USD 586 billion in 2026, the market is projected to reach nearly USD 992 billion by 2035, growing at a CAGR of about 6%. This growth is largely driven by rising global obesity rates and increasing acceptance of body-positive fashion. With more than 2.1 billion adults classified as overweight and over 690 million obese, demand for extended-size clothing has become a major opportunity for apparel brands. Today, a significant portion of consumers require larger sizes, with many retailers reporting that plus-size collections contribute a substantial share of overall apparel sales. As a result, both mainstream fashion labels and specialized brands are expanding their product lines to cater to this growing segment.

The market is segmented across different product types and consumer groups. Women dominate the demand, accounting for about 62% of total plus-size apparel purchases, followed by men at 31% and children at 7%. Among product categories, casual wear leads with around 34% of sales, including everyday clothing such as jeans, T-shirts, and tops. Other important segments include sportswear (18%), innerwear (14%), formal wear (12%), ethnic wear (11%), and nightwear (7%). The popularity of sportswear and athleisure is increasing as more consumers seek comfortable and functional clothing for active lifestyles. Additionally, innovations in fabric technology, such as stretch materials, breathable textiles, and adjustable fits, are helping brands design garments that better accommodate diverse body shapes and improve customer satisfaction.

Regionally, North America holds the largest share of the global plus-size clothing market with about 39%, followed by Europe with 29%, Asia-Pacific with 23%, and the Middle East & Africa with 9%. North America leads due to high demand, strong retail networks, and widespread body-positive marketing campaigns. Meanwhile, Asia-Pacific is emerging as a fast-growing region because of rapid urbanization, rising disposable income, and expanding e-commerce platforms. The competitive landscape includes major fashion brands such as Lane Bryant, H&M, Nike, ASOS, Adidas, Puma, and Mango, along with many independent and digital-first labels. As brands continue investing in inclusive designs, e-commerce expansion, and advanced sizing technologies, the plus-size clothing industry is expected to grow further, offering new opportunities for retailers and manufacturers worldwide.
[16/03/26, 1:36:39 AM] Lalith: The Indian fashion industry is gradually recognizing the importance of size inclusivity as awareness about body diversity increases. Traditionally, fashion in India has been strongly influenced by media and entertainment industries that promote slimmer body standards, which has often left plus-size consumers feeling excluded from mainstream fashion spaces. Many individuals struggle to find clothing that fits well and reflects their personal style due to limited size ranges, poor design considerations, and societal biases. Since clothing plays an important role in self-expression and confidence, these limitations create both practical and emotional challenges for plus-size shoppers.

Despite these challenges, the plus-size fashion segment shows strong potential for growth in India. Changing consumer attitudes, especially among younger generations such as Gen Z, are encouraging brands to adopt more inclusive approaches. Social media platforms have played a significant role in promoting body positivity and challenging unrealistic beauty standards. Influencers and consumers are increasingly advocating for fashion that represents real bodies. At the same time, e-commerce platforms have expanded access to extended sizes and made it easier for consumers to discover inclusive brands.

Events such as Lakmé Fashion Week have begun to reflect small shifts toward inclusivity, with curvier models and body-positivity advocates appearing on the runway. While this representation is encouraging, it has also raised questions about whether these efforts represent genuine change or symbolic gestures. Experts argue that many designers still misunderstand the needs of plus-size consumers by offering limited silhouettes or basic designs instead of stylish and diverse options. Another major issue is the absence of a standardized Indian sizing system, which often leads to inconsistent fits because many brands rely on Western size measurements that do not accurately reflect Indian body proportions.

In addition to design challenges, plus-size consumers frequently encounter barriers while shopping. Many customers report higher prices for larger sizes, sometimes referred to as a “fat tax,” along with uncomfortable experiences in physical stores. The lack of size-diverse models in advertising and limited trial samples also make it difficult for consumers to visualize how clothing will look on their bodies. Although some brands are beginning to address these issues by offering extended size ranges, customized garments, and inclusive campaigns, true progress requires deeper commitment from designers and retailers.

The growth of digital platforms has played a crucial role in improving access to inclusive fashion. Online retail has allowed consumers from Tier-II and Tier-III cities to explore brands that offer extended sizes and better-fitting clothing. As India’s fashion and lifestyle sector continues to expand, many shoppers are beginning to find stylish outfits that accommodate diverse body shapes. However, producing clothing in a wider range of sizes can increase manufacturing complexity and costs, and cultural attitudes in some regions still limit widespread acceptance of plus-size fashion.

Globally, the plus-size clothing market is also experiencing significant expansion. The market is valued at approximately USD 586 billion in 2026 and is projected to reach nearly USD 992 billion by 2035, growing steadily due to increasing awareness of body diversity and rising demand for inclusive fashion. Women account for the largest share of this market, followed by men and children. Casual wear represents the largest product segment, while sportswear, innerwear, formal wear, ethnic wear, and nightwear also contribute significantly to overall sales.

Regionally, North America holds the largest share of the global plus-size apparel market, followed by Europe and the Asia-Pacific region. However, Asia-Pacific—including India—is emerging as one of the fastest-growing markets due to rapid urbanization, rising incomes, and expanding e-commerce. As major global brands and independent labels invest in inclusive design, advanced sizing technologies, and online retail expansion, the plus-size fashion industry is expected to continue growing. With genuine commitment to inclusivity, the fashion industry has the potential to create a more welcoming environment where clothing provides comfort, confidence, and self-expression for people of all body types.

[17/03/26, 2:20:44 AM] Lalith: Slide 1: Title Slide
Plus-Size Fashion Industry in India
Challenges, Opportunities, and Market Growth
The fashion industry has traditionally focused on limited body sizes, leaving many consumers underserved.
With the rise of the body positivity movement, the demand for inclusive fashion has increased.
Plus-size fashion is now becoming an important social and economic trend in the global fashion industry.
This presentation explores the current situation, market growth, challenges, and opportunities in the plus-size fashion sector.
Slide 2: Introduction
Fashion plays an important role in self-expression, identity, and confidence.
Historically, fashion brands produced clothing based on standardized body measurements, often ignoring body diversity.
As a result, many plus-size individuals struggle to find stylish, comfortable, and well-fitting clothing.
The body positivity movement, supported by social media and influencers, has encouraged brands to rethink traditional beauty standards.
Today, consumers are demanding greater inclusivity and representation in fashion.
Slide 3: Importance of Inclusive Fashion
Inclusive fashion promotes representation and acceptance of diverse body types.
It allows individuals to feel confident and comfortable in their clothing choices.
Inclusive fashion also has significant economic potential, as a large portion of consumers fall into the plus-size category.
Brands that adopt inclusive design strategies can increase customer satisfaction and build long-term loyalty.
Inclusivity is becoming an important part of modern brand identity and social responsibility.
Slide 4: Current Situation in India
In India, many fashion brands still offer sizes only up to L or XL, limiting options for plus-size consumers.
Plus-size clothing is often designed as oversized garments rather than stylish, fitted outfits.
Representation of diverse body types in advertising campaigns and runway shows remains limited.
However, awareness about body diversity and inclusive fashion is increasing among younger consumers.
Events such as inclusive fashion shows and body-positive campaigns are slowly changing the industry.
Slide 5: Cultural and Media Influence
Media and entertainment industries often promote slim body standards, influencing perceptions of beauty.
This creates social pressure for individuals to conform to narrow body ideals.
Social media platforms like Instagram and YouTube have enabled body-positive influencers to challenge these stereotypes.
Fashion brands are increasingly collaborating with diverse models and influencers to promote inclusivity.
As representation improves, consumer attitudes toward body diversity are gradually changing.
Slide 6: Consumer Challenges
One major issue is inconsistent sizing across different brands, which makes shopping confusing.
India lacks a standardized sizing system that accurately reflects local body proportions.
Plus-size customers often find limited trial options in physical stores, making it difficult to choose the right fit.
Some consumers report being charged higher prices for larger sizes, sometimes referred to as a “fat tax.”
Social stigma and negative experiences in stores can also discourage plus-size shoppers.
Slide 7: Global Plus-Size Market Growth
Key statistics
Global plus-size clothing market value: $586 billion in 2026
Expected to reach $992 billion by 2035
Estimated annual growth rate: around 6% CAGR
Reasons for growth:
Increasing awareness about body diversity
Rising demand for inclusive clothing options
Growth of e-commerce platforms
Graph (Line Chart)
Year	Market Size
2026	$586B
2030	$700B
2035	$992B
Slide 8: Product Categories in Plus-Size Fashion
The plus-size fashion market includes several product categories:
Category	Market Share
Casual Wear	34%
Sportswear	18%
Innerwear	14%
Formal Wear	12%
Ethnic Wear	11%
Nightwear	7%
Casual wear dominates because it includes everyday clothing such as jeans, tops, and T-shirts.
Sportswear and athleisure are growing rapidly due to increasing interest in fitness and comfort.
Graph
Pie Chart showing category distribution.
Slide 9: Regional Market Distribution
The global plus-size market is distributed across different regions.
Region	Market Share
North America	39%
Europe	29%
Asia-Pacific	23%
Middle East & Africa	9%
North America leads due to strong retail networks and body-positive campaigns.
Asia-Pacific, including India, is experiencing rapid growth due to urbanization and e-commerce expansion.
Graph
Pie Chart showing regional distribution.
Slide 10: Consumer Segments
The plus-size fashion market can also be divided by consumer groups.
Segment	Share
Women	62%
Men	31%
Children	7%
Women dominate the market due to greater demand for variety and fashion trends.
Men’s plus-size clothing is also growing with increasing awareness and availability.
The children’s segment remains smaller but is gradually expanding.
Graph
Pie Chart showing consumer segments.
Slide 11: Role of E-Commerce
Online shopping platforms have made it easier for consumers to access extended-size clothing.
Customers can explore a wider variety of brands and styles than what is available in physical stores.
E-commerce platforms also provide customer reviews and size guides, helping consumers choose better-fitting clothing.
Online marketplaces have significantly improved access for consumers in smaller cities.
Slide 12: Technology and Innovation
New fabric technologies such as stretchable and breathable materials improve comfort and fit.
Virtual try-on technology allows customers to visualize how clothing will look before purchasing.
Data-driven sizing systems help brands design more accurate and inclusive clothing sizes.
Innovations in fashion technology are helping solve many fit and comfort issues.
Slide 13: Opportunities for Brands
The plus-size market represents a large and growing consumer segment.
Brands that offer inclusive designs can gain competitive advantages.
Collaborating with body-positive influencers can improve brand visibility.
Inclusive marketing campaigns can build trust and loyalty among consumers.
Slide 14: Future Trends in Plus-Size Fashion
More brands are expected to introduce extended size ranges.
Inclusive fashion shows and campaigns are becoming more common.
Sustainable and inclusive fashion may become key industry trends.
Increasing representation of diverse body types in media will continue to transform the fashion industry.
Slide 15: Conclusion
Plus-size fashion is both a social movement and a significant business opportunity.
The industry must improve design strategies, sizing systems, and representation.
Inclusive fashion can help individuals feel confident, comfortable, and represented.
The future of fashion should focus on diversity, inclusivity, and accessibility for everyone.

[17/03/26, 10:14:37 PM] Buddankay: The research paper titled “A Study on Factors Influencing Plus Size Fashion Apparel” examines the factors that influence the purchasing behavior and satisfaction of plus-size clothing consumers in India. Plus-size clothing refers to garments designed for individuals who require larger sizes than the standard clothing range. The study highlights that the plus-size apparel market was neglected for many years but has recently gained attention due to the increasing demand for fashionable clothing for larger body types. Customer satisfaction plays an important role in the clothing consumption process, which includes purchasing, using, storing, and eventually discarding apparel. The study explains that many plus-size consumers feel dissatisfied with clothing due to issues such as poor fitting, lack of style, limited variety, and fewer alternatives available in the market.

The research uses both quantitative and qualitative approaches to understand consumer behavior. Data was collected using questionnaires distributed to several respondents in order to analyze their shopping satisfaction, buying satisfaction, and consuming satisfaction. The study examined how these factors influence the decision of plus-size customers to wear, keep, or discard clothing. The results show that many consumers purchase plus-size clothing based on fashion trends, color choices, comfort, fitting, and brand reputation. According to the findings, a large percentage of respondents prefer fashionable and trendy clothing, and they often visit specialized plus-size stores because regular stores do not provide suitable options. However, some consumers feel hesitant to purchase plus-size clothing due to privacy concerns and fear of body shaming while shopping in stores.

The study concludes that factors such as fashion trends, variety of clothing, comfort, pricing, and store environment significantly influence the buying decisions of plus-size consumers. Many respondents prefer customized clothing rather than ready-made garments because it provides better fitting and comfort. The research also found that the price of plus-size clothing is often higher than regular clothing, which negatively affects customer satisfaction. Therefore, retailers and designers should focus on offering a wider variety of fashionable designs, affordable pricing, and better store layouts that respect customer privacy. By improving these aspects, companies can better serve the growing plus-size fashion market and increase customer satisfaction in this segment.
[17/03/26, 10:14:37 PM] Buddankay: The dissertation “Evaluating the Plus Size Body: Advertisement and Exploitation in the Media and the Fashion Industry” examines how plus-size bodies are represented in the media and fashion industries. The research explores whether the inclusion of plus-size models in advertisements is a genuine attempt to promote body diversity or simply a marketing strategy used by brands to attract attention and profit. In recent years, the visibility of plus-size bodies in advertising and social media has increased, but the study questions whether this representation is meaningful or only superficial. The research focuses on how brands present plus-size models in advertising campaigns, websites, and social media platforms to understand whether their actions truly support body inclusivity.

The study also discusses how media and consumer culture have historically shaped ideas about the “ideal body.” For many years, the fashion industry promoted extremely thin body types as the standard of beauty. Over time, body trends changed and curvier bodies began to appear more frequently in media and popular culture. However, the research argues that even when curvier bodies are represented, they often still reflect a modified version of the thin ideal rather than true body diversity. Advertising, magazines, and social media platforms play a major role in spreading these body ideals, which can influence how women perceive their own bodies and encourage them to compare themselves with unrealistic images.

The dissertation concludes that although plus-size representation in fashion and media has improved, it is still limited and sometimes misleading. Many brands include plus-size models in their campaigns but fail to provide clothing in extended sizes or do not consistently promote body diversity in their products. In some cases, companies use the body positivity movement mainly as a marketing tool rather than a genuine effort to support inclusivity. The research suggests that greater transparency and more authentic representation of diverse body types are needed in the fashion industry so that plus-size individuals can feel truly included and represented in media and advertising.
[17/03/26, 10:14:37 PM] Buddankay: The research article “One Size Does Not Fit All: Optimizing Size-Inclusive Model Photography Mitigates Fit Risk in Online Fashion Retailing” examines how the body size of fashion models used in online shopping websites affects consumers’ purchasing decisions. The study explains that most online fashion retailers still use very thin models to display clothing items. However, these images often fail to represent the body shapes of many consumers. This situation can negatively influence how shoppers perceive their own bodies and may also affect their confidence when buying clothes online. Since customers cannot physically try on garments when shopping online, they rely heavily on model images to imagine how the clothes might look on them. The research therefore focuses on how different model body sizes influence customers’ perception of clothing fit and their willingness to purchase products.

The study introduces the concept called the “Dissimilarity-Risk Deterrence Effect.” This idea explains that when consumers see clothing displayed on models whose body size is very different from their own, they feel less confident about how the clothing will fit them. As a result, they perceive a higher risk of poor fit and become less likely to purchase the product. Through multiple studies and experiments, the researchers found that when clothing is shown on models with body sizes similar to the consumer’s own body size, customers feel more confident in judging the product fit. This similarity helps them imagine how the clothing might look on their body, which reduces uncertainty and increases purchase intention.

The findings suggest that using diverse body sizes in fashion model photography can benefit both consumers and retailers. Showing clothing on models of different sizes can make online shopping more inclusive and help customers make better purchasing decisions. It can also reduce product returns, which often occur when customers receive clothing that does not fit as expected. Therefore, the study concludes that fashion brands and online retailers should adopt size-inclusive marketing strategies by displaying clothing on models with varied body types. This approach can improve customer satisfaction, increase sales, and promote a more inclusive representation of body diversity in the fashion industry.
[17/03/26, 10:14:37 PM] Buddankay: The study titled “Exploring Influential Factors on Customer Satisfaction with Clothing for Overweight and Obese Women: A Focus on Generations Y and Z in Sri Lanka” examines the factors that influence clothing satisfaction among overweight and obese women aged between 20 and 35 years in Sri Lanka. The global textile and clothing industry has grown rapidly, and the demand for plus-size clothing is increasing as the number of overweight and obese individuals rises worldwide. Despite this growing demand, many fashion retailers still provide limited options for plus-size women, leading to dissatisfaction related to clothing fit, style, and availability. The study highlights that clothing plays an important role in shaping self-confidence, identity, and social perception. However, women with larger body sizes often struggle to find fashionable clothing that fits well, which can lead to frustration and feelings of exclusion from the fashion industry.

To understand the determinants of clothing satisfaction, the research is based on Renoux’s theory of retail satisfaction, which includes three major components: the shopping system, buying system, and consuming system. The shopping system focuses on aspects before purchase, such as store accessibility, proper information about plus-size clothing, and the presence of mannequins that represent larger body types. The buying system involves factors during the purchasing process, including the availability of different sizes, stylish designs, reasonable price ranges, high-quality materials, and spacious fitting rooms. The consuming system refers to the experience after purchase, including body proportion, body image, comfort, and how well the clothing fits the consumer’s body. The study collected data using a questionnaire from overweight and obese women across different provinces in Sri Lanka and analyzed the results through statistical techniques such as multiple regression analysis.

The findings reveal that the shopping system and buying system have a significant positive influence on clothing satisfaction among overweight and obese women, with the buying system having the strongest impact. Factors such as having a separate section for plus-size clothing, displaying plus-size mannequins, offering trendy designs, maintaining reasonable prices, and providing comfortable fitting rooms can greatly improve customer satisfaction. However, the consuming system was found to have no significant statistical influence on satisfaction in this study. The research concludes that fashion retailers should pay greater attention to the needs of plus-size consumers by improving store environments, offering diverse and fashionable clothing options, and ensuring better accessibility and information. These improvements can enhance the shopping experience and help plus-size women feel more confident, comfortable, and included in the fashion market.
[17/03/26, 10:14:37 PM] Buddankay: The thesis “Body Inclusivity: The Effect of Size Availability on the Well-Being of Different Size Young Women” examines how clothing size availability in the fashion industry influences young women’s emotions, body image, and overall well-being. The study explains that clothing is closely connected to a person’s body image and self-perception. When young women cannot find clothes that fit their bodies, they may feel dissatisfied with themselves and believe that their bodies do not match society’s “ideal” appearance. Fashion advertisements often promote thin and attractive models, which can create unrealistic beauty standards. As a result, many young women compare themselves with these idealized images and may develop lower self-esteem, negative emotions, and dissatisfaction with their body shape.

The research also highlights the growing importance of body positivity and size inclusivity in the fashion industry. The body positivity movement encourages acceptance of different body shapes and challenges unrealistic beauty ideals promoted by media and fashion brands. However, even though many companies claim to support body inclusivity, true representation is still limited. Many clothing brands offer only a narrow range of sizes, and plus-size options are often unavailable in physical stores. The study discusses how inclusive sizing and representation in advertising can influence consumers’ feelings of belonging, emotional responses, and purchasing decisions. Young women are more likely to feel included and confident when they see models with body types similar to their own and when clothing sizes are available for a wider range of body shapes.

To investigate these effects, the researcher conducted a quantitative study using an experimental design where participants were shown different models (thin and plus-size) and different levels of size availability (inclusive and exclusive). The results indicated that the similarity between consumers and the models had a significant influence on purchase intention. When consumers felt that the model’s body type was similar to their own and when inclusive sizes were available, they showed a greater willingness to purchase the clothing. Although some expected effects such as emotional response and well-being were not statistically significant, the research still emphasizes the importance of size inclusivity in improving shopping experiences and consumer satisfaction. The study concludes that fashion brands should expand size ranges and represent diverse body types to promote inclusivity and improve the confidence and well-being of young women.

[17/03/26, 10:28:10 PM] Lalith: India’s fashion industry is slowly starting to realise that style shouldn’t really depend on body size. Even though the global body positivity movement has pushed brands to be more inclusive, in India the progress still feels uneven. A lot of plus-size shoppers still struggle to find clothes that actually fit well or look good. Most of the time, options are limited, designs feel outdated, and there’s still this strong idea of what an “ideal” body should look like.

Cultural influence plays a big role here. If you notice movies, advertisements, or even social media, the same slim body type keeps getting promoted again and again. Because of that, many people feel like they don’t really belong in mainstream fashion. And in a country like India, where clothing is closely connected to identity and culture, that can feel quite frustrating.

But yeah, things are changing, just not very fast. The plus-size market is growing, and more people—especially Gen Z—are starting to speak up about it. Social media is one of the main reasons for this change. Just scrolling through Instagram or YouTube, you can notice how people are more open about body image and share their personal experiences. It doesn’t feel as perfect or edited as it used to.

Also, online shopping has helped in a practical way. People now have more options and a better chance of finding sizes that actually work for them, which wasn’t always the case earlier.

Even now, what brands show and what they actually offer don’t always match. You may see some plus-size models in ads or shows, but when you actually try to shop, the options are still not that many. On top of that, sizing is inconsistent. Something that fits well in one brand might not fit the same in another, which gets frustrating over time.

That said, progress is happening. In cities like Bengaluru, Mumbai, and Delhi, more people are finally finding clothes that actually suit them. For many people in smaller cities, online platforms have honestly changed the whole experience of shopping.

Recently, people have started caring more about how clothes feel when they wear them, not only about appearance. In a place like India, where styles range from sarees to kurtas, it becomes important to think about different body types while designing. It’s not always easy for brands to manage this, but there’s clearly a lot of scope if they get it right.

If you look at the bigger picture, the plus-size fashion market is expanding steadily across the world and doesn’t seem to be slowing down anytime soon. Women still make up the biggest share, and casual wear continues to be the most popular category. North America might still be ahead right now, but regions like Asia-Pacific—especially India—are gradually closing that gap.

In the end, this goes beyond just clothing. It’s more about feeling okay with yourself and not feeling excluded. If brands begin to understand this properly, fashion in India can gradually become more inclusive for everyone.

‎[17/03/26, 10:40:40 PM] Lalith: ‎audio omitted
[17/03/26, 10:40:46 PM] Lalith: These studies are mainly about how the fashion industry affects young women, especially when it comes to body size and clothing choices.

Clothing is not only about looking good, it also affects how a person feels about themselves. When young women don’t find clothes that fit them properly, they may feel uncomfortable or even think their body is not “right.” This mostly happens because fashion advertisements usually show very slim models, and that creates a kind of ideal body image. Many women end up comparing themselves with these images, which can reduce their confidence.

Even though body positivity is being talked about more these days, in reality, many brands are still not fully inclusive. They may show diversity in ads, but when it comes to actual sizes in stores, the options are limited. Because of this, many women still feel left out.

Another important thing is the shopping experience. For plus-size women, satisfaction depends on simple things like whether their size is available, if the clothes are stylish, and if prices are reasonable. Even things like having a proper fitting room or a separate section can make shopping easier and more comfortable. Sometimes, people prefer custom-made clothes because ready-made ones don’t fit well.

In online shopping, most clothes are shown on thin models, which makes it difficult for customers to imagine how the clothes will look on them. This creates doubt and sometimes people avoid buying. But when brands use models with different body types, it helps customers feel more confident.

Also, even though plus-size representation has increased, it is not always genuine. Some brands just use it for promotion but don’t actually provide better size options.
